title: AES round datapath — worst slack on state register
description:
# AES round datapath

You've inherited an AES-128 round implementation during timing closure. Synthesis reports a **setup violation** on the state register feedback path at 1.0 GHz (1000 ps period). The violation is -180 ps on the worst path from `state_reg[7]/Q` to `state_reg[3]/D`.

The design went through synthesis with default effort. Clock uncertainty is 50 ps, insertion delay is 100 ps. No timing exceptions are currently defined.

**Your job:** find the root cause and apply a fix. You have a restricted command palette — think before you spend investigation budget.

reportedViolation.summary: setup VIOLATED (-0.180 ns) on state_reg_7/Q -> state_reg_3/D

rootCauseOptions.label: sbox_mux drives a high-capacitance net into a heavy LUT input (n_mux_y has 85 ps of wire delay and feeds a 0.004 pF pin)
Clock skew between state_reg_7 and state_reg_3
Missing false_path on the state feedback loop

acceptedFixes.description: Size sbox_mux from MUX4X1 to MUX4X4 to drive n_mux_y faster
Insert a BUFX4 on n_mux_y between sbox_mux and sbox_lut
Apply useful skew: delay capture clock on state_reg_3 by 100 ps

hintTree.nudge: Before changing anything, look at where the delay is accumulating. Which single segment of the data path contributes the most?
hintTree.hint: Compare cell delays (arcs) vs net delays. You should see one net with unusually high wire delay — investigate what's driving it and what it's feeding.
hintTree.walkthrough: Run `report_timing -from state_reg_7/Q -to state_reg_3/D`. Walk the path: the sbox_mux -> sbox_lut segment has 85 ps of wire delay on n_mux_y, which is dominant. `get_nets -pattern n_mux_y` confirms high capacitance (0.028 pF). The MUX4X1 driver is too weak. Pick size_up_mux (best slack recovery) or buffer_insert (smaller PPA hit).

steps.title: Understand the violation
steps.goal: Read the timing report for the failing path. Identify the startpoint, endpoint, and how much slack is missing.

checkpoint.question: What is the data arrival time on this path?

steps.insight: The data arrives at ~1.44 ns but is required by ~0.96 ns. That's a 480 ps overshoot in a 1 ns clock — nearly half a cycle of excess. The path is long.

steps.title: Identify the dominant delay segment
steps.goal: Walk the timing report line by line. Find which cell arc or net contributes the most incremental delay.

checkpoint.question: Which net has the highest wire delay?

steps.insight: n_mux_y has 85 ps of wire delay — more than any single cell arc except sbox_lut. Its capacitance is 0.028 pF, which is 2x the next-largest net. The driver (sbox_mux MUX4X1) is struggling.

steps.title: Check the clock network
steps.goal: Rule out clock skew as a contributing factor. Compare insertion delays at launch and capture flops.

checkpoint.question: Is clock skew a significant contributor to this violation?

options.label: No — both flops have similar insertion delay
Yes — capture flop clock arrives much later

steps.insight: Both state_reg_7 and state_reg_3 are on the same clock with near-zero skew. This is purely a data-path problem.

steps.title: Investigate the bottleneck driver
steps.goal: Look at sbox_mux — the cell driving n_mux_y. Check its type and what it's driving into.

options.label: MUX4X1 is too weak to drive n_mux_y's 0.028 pF load
sbox_lut has too much intrinsic delay
key_xor at the end of the chain is the bottleneck

steps.insight: MUX4X1 (X1 drive strength) is pushing 0.028 pF across a 72-ohm wire. That R*C product creates the dominant 85 ps. Sizing up the mux or buffering the net are the canonical fixes.
